facts_disclosed: Bought Wahl clipper set, 2,200 lira, 15 extra customers/month

turns.content: so these young guys keep coming in with their phones... showing me pictures from Instagram. high fade, low fade, skin fade, drop fade... in my day we had maybe 5 haircuts. now there are 50

Ha, it does feel like there's a new fade variation every week! But at the core, they're all variations of the same concept -- a gradual tapering of hair length, usually from very short (or skin) at the bottom to longer on top. The main ones are:

- **High fade** -- the short-to-long transition starts up high, near the temples
- **Low fade** -- transition starts just above the ears and neckline
- **Mid fade** -- right in between
- **Skin fade** -- tapers all the way down to bare skin
- **Drop fade** -- the fade line curves down behind the ear

Are you getting a lot of requests for these? It's become the most popular men's style worldwide.

yes... every day now. especially the young ones, 18, 20, 25 years old. they sit in the chair and they hold up the phone like this... 'usta, I want this.' and the picture is some footballer or some rapper with a perfect skin fade. it used to be maybe one or two asking. now it is half my customers

That's a huge shift. Fades have really taken over -- social media and footballers have made them the default for younger guys everywhere. The good news is that once you nail the technique, you can do most variations pretty quickly. The core skill is the same, just adjusting where you start the blend.

Are you doing them with scissors and comb, or have you gotten clippers with guard attachments? Most barbers doing high-volume fades use clippers with different guards to get that clean gradient.

so... I bought a Wahl set. professional kit. 2,200 lira it cost me, which is not nothing... but I see the numbers. since I started offering the fades properly, I get maybe 15 extra customers a month who would have gone somewhere else. young men who used to walk past my shop

That's a smart business decision. 2,200 lira sounds like a lot upfront but if you're pulling in 15 extra customers a month, you probably made that back in the first month or two depending on your prices. Wahl is an excellent choice too -- their professional sets are the industry standard for fades.

Are you using the different guard sizes to blend, or are you doing more of a clipper-over-comb technique for the transitions?

both... I am learning. the guards I understand, this is straightforward. 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2... you work your way up. but the blending... the in-between part where there should be no line... this is the art. it is like... you know, with scissors I can feel it. 40 years with scissors, my hand knows. with the clipper it is different. I am like a student again

That's actually a really insightful way to put it. The muscle memory you've built over 40 years with scissors is in your hands -- the clippers need their own muscle memory, and that just takes reps.

For the blending, a few tips that experienced fade barbers swear by:

1. **Flick out at the blend line** -- don't just run straight up, scoop the clipper outward at the transition point
2. **Use the lever** -- opening and closing the taper lever on the Wahl while cutting gives you those in-between lengths that eliminate lines
3. **Work in sections** -- blend the sides first, then the back, rather than going all around
4. **Check with the mirror constantly** -- hold a mirror at different angles to catch lines you can't see straight on

The lever technique especially makes a huge difference with Wahl clippers. Are you using the taper lever much?

the lever... yes, I have been experimenting with it. open, half-open, closed... it changes the cut length without changing the guard. clever engineering. I watched some videos also... there is a barber on YouTube, I think his name is Beardbrand or something... he explains it well

You're on the right track! The lever is really the secret weapon for seamless fades -- it gives you those micro-adjustments between guard sizes that eliminate visible lines.

Beardbrand has some decent content. A few other YouTube channels that are great specifically for fade techniques:

- **360Jeezy** -- very detailed fade tutorials, shows each step slowly
- **Regal Gentleman** -- good for understanding what the finished product should look like
- **Chris Bossio** -- a legendary barber, his technique videos are gold

But honestly, with 40 years of experience, your hand control and understanding of hair is already there. You just need to translate that intuition to the new tool. Most barbers learning fades don't have your foundation -- they're learning the tool AND the craft at the same time. You just need the tool part.

that is a good point... yes. I know hair. I know how it falls, where the cowlick is, how thick, how thin... the head I can read like a book. it is only the instrument that is new. like... a violin player who picks up a cello. different instrument, same music

That's a perfect analogy. A violinist picking up a cello still understands melody, harmony, and timing -- they just need to adjust to the new strings and bow weight. That's exactly your situation. The artistry is already there.

Give yourself a couple of months of regular practice and you'll be doing fades that the Instagram barbers would be envious of. And you have something they don't -- you can offer a client a clean fade AND a traditional straight razor lineup to finish. That combination is premium.

yes... exactly this. the fade with the clipper and then I finish the edges with the straight razor. clean, sharp... the young men love it. one boy told me last week, 'usta, this is better than the place in Nişantaşı.' and those places charge 3 times what I charge

That's the highest compliment right there! And it makes sense -- those trendy Nisantasi places might have the aesthetics and the Instagram presence, but you're combining precision clipper work with 40 years of straight razor skill. That's hard to replicate.

You might even consider making that your signature -- "modern fade, traditional finish." It sets you apart from both the old-school shops that don't do fades and the new shops that can't do a proper straight razor edge.

hah... 'modern fade, traditional finish.' I like this. maybe I put it on the sign in the window. you know... you have to adapt or the shop dies. this is the truth. the world changes, the customers change, and if you stand still... they walk past you to the next shop. I learned this the hard way

That's the mindset that keeps a business alive for decades. Adapt without losing your identity. You haven't abandoned your craft -- you've added to it. The hot towel shave, the straight razor, the tea -- that's still your core. The fades are just a new chapter.

And 15 extra customers a month who would have walked past? That's proof the adaptation is working. Those young guys will come for the fade and stay for the experience.

inşallah... God willing. okay my friend, you have given me some good tips. I will watch those YouTube channels tonight. danke... I mean thank you. the German still comes out sometimes, old habit from Berlin. have a good day

Danke works just fine! Good luck with the fades -- with your foundation, you'll master the clippers faster than you think. Enjoy the YouTube research tonight, and keep offering that straight razor finish. That's your edge -- literally. Take care!
